competition: 55rd Ukrainian National Mathematical Olympiad - Third Round
gold_final_answer: a) 2103, 2104, 2105, 2106, 2107, 2108, 2109; b) No, a chain longer than seven consecutive colored years cannot occur.
topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Coloring schemes, extremal arguments
prompt:
Let us call a year *colored* if the decimal representation of its number has no repeating digits. For example, all years from $2013$ to $2019$ are colored, unlike $2020$.

a) Find the nearest chain of seven consecutive colored years in the future.

b) Can a chain of more than seven consecutive years happen in the future?
response_text:
A year is **colored** if its decimal digits are all distinct.

We are asked:

- find the nearest future block of **7 consecutive colored years**;
- decide whether a block of length **more than 7** can ever occur.

We interpret “in the future” relative to the example around 2013–2019, so we seek the first such block after that.

## Key observation
In any block of 10 consecutive years, the last digit runs through all values \(0,1,\dots,9\).  
So if the first digits are fixed, one of those 10 years will usually repeat a digit and fail to be colored.

For years in the 2000s, every year has the form \(20ab\), so digit \(2\) and digit \(0\) are already present. Thus \(a,b\) must be distinct and also different from \(2,0\).

For years \(20xy\), if we want several consecutive years, only the last digit changes most of the time.

---

## a) Nearest chain of seven consecutive colored years

We check around 2013–2019:

- 2013, 2014, 2015, 2016, 2017, 2018, 2019 are all colored.
- 2012 is not colored (repeats 2),
- 2020 is not colored (repeats 2 and 0).

So this is a chain of seven consecutive colored years.

To see it is the nearest such chain in the future: starting from 1988 up to 2012, no block of 7 occurs crossing 2000 because all years 2000–2012 contain repeated digits:
- 2000–2009 all repeat 0,
- 2010, 2011, 2012 repeat 0,1,2 respectively.

Thus the first future block of 7 consecutive colored years is

\[
\boxed{2013,2014,2015,2016,2017,2018,2019}.
\]

---

## b) Can there be more than seven consecutive colored years?

We show **no**.

Consider any year \(N\).

### Case 1: the block does not cross a tens boundary
Then the first three digits stay fixed, and only the units digit changes through consecutive values.  
Among any 8 consecutive integers, the units digits are 8 consecutive residues mod 10. If the fixed first three digits already contain some digit \(d\), then the year ending in \(d\) is not colored. Also if one of the fixed digits repeats another fixed digit, then none are colored anyway.

So within such a stretch, at least one year fails.

### Cleaner direct argument
Take any 8 consecutive years. They lie either

- entirely within one decade, say \(abc0,abc1,\dots,abc9\), or
- they cross a decade boundary.

If they lie within one decade, the first three digits \(a,b,c\) are fixed. Since \(c\) is one of \(0,\dots,9\), the year ending in digit \(c\) occurs in that decade, namely \(abcc\), and this year is not colored. Hence no 8 consecutive years in one decade can all be colored.

If they cross a boundary, then among them there is a year ending in \(0\). Such a year has form \(abc0\). If any of \(a,b,c\) is \(0\), it is not colored; if not, then looking near the boundary we also encounter a year where the last digit equals one of \(a,b,c\), again causing repetition. More simply, any set of 8 consecutive years is contained in some set of 10 consecutive years with fixed first three digits except possibly at one boundary, and among those 10 there must be a year whose last digit matches one of the earlier digits, so that year is not colored.

A more concrete way: for a fixed thousand/hundred/ten prefix, among the 10 possibilities for the last digit, at most \(10-3=7\) can avoid matching one of the first three digits. Therefore within any decade there are at most 7 colored years. Hence a run of 8 consecutive colored years is impossible.

Thus a chain longer than 7 cannot occur.

And since the example 2013–2019 gives a chain of length 7, the maximum possible length is exactly 7.

Final answer: a) \(2013\)–\(2019\). b) No; more than seven consecutive colored years are impossible.
